--- title: Makie subtitle: A data visualization ecosystem for Julia author: Marie-Hélène Burle --- :::{.def} *Content from [the webinar slides](wb_makie_slides.qmd) for easier browsing.* ::: ## Plotting in Julia There are many options to create plots in Julia. Some of the most popular ones are: - [Plots.jl](https://github.com/JuliaPlots/Plots.jl): high-level API for working with different back-ends (GR, Pyplot, Plotly...), - [PyPlot.jl](https://github.com/JuliaPy/PyPlot.jl): Julia interface to Matplotlib's `matplotlib.pyplot`, - [PlotlyJS.jl](https://github.com/JuliaPlots/PlotlyJS.jl): Julia interface to plotly.js, - [PlotlyLight.jl](https://github.com/JuliaComputing/PlotlyLight.jl): the fastest plotting option in Julia by far, but limited features, - [Gadfly.jl](https://github.com/GiovineItalia/Gadfly.jl): following the grammar of graphics popularized by Hadley Wickham in R, - [VegaLite.jl](https://github.com/queryverse/VegaLite.jl): grammar of interactive graphics, - [PGFPlotsX.jl](https://github.com/KristofferC/PGFPlotsX.jl): Julia interface to the PGFPlots LaTeX package, - [UnicodePlots.jl](https://github.com/JuliaPlots/UnicodePlots.jl): plots in the terminal 🙂, - [Makie.jl](https://github.com/MakieOrg/Makie.jl): powerful plotting ecosystem: animation, 3D, GPU optimization. This webinar focuses on [Makie.jl](https://github.com/MakieOrg/Makie.jl). ## The Makie ecosystem Makie consists of a core package (`Makie`), with the plots functionalities. In addition to this, a backend is needed to render plots into images or vector graphics. Three backends are available: - `CairoMakie`: vector graphics or high-quality 2D plots. Creates, but does not display plots (you need an IDE that does or you can use [ElectronDisplay.jl](https://github.com/queryverse/ElectronDisplay.jl)), - `GLMakie`: based on [OpenGL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenGL); 3D rendering and interactivity in [GLFW](https://www.glfw.org/) window (no vector graphics), - `WGLMakie`: web version of `GLMakie` (plots rendered in a browser instead of a window). ## Resources Here are some links and resources useful to get started with the Makie ecosystem: - [the official Makie documentation](https://docs.makie.org/stable/), - the project [Beautiful Makie](https://github.com/MakieOrg/BeautifulMakie) contains many great plot examples, - cheatsheets: for 2D plotting: ![From: [Storopoli, Huijzer and Alonso (2021). Julia Data Science. ISBN: 97984898](https://juliadatascience.io/datavisMakie_attributes)](img/makiecheatsheet2D.png){width=85%} for 3D plotting: ![From: [Storopoli, Huijzer and Alonso (2021). Julia Data Science. ISBN: 97984898](https://juliadatascience.io/datavisMakie_attributes)](img/makiecheatsheet3D.png) ## Troubleshooting CairoMakie and WGLMakie should install without issues. Installing GLMakie however can be challenging. [This page](https://github.com/MakieOrg/Makie.jl/tree/master/GLMakie#troubleshooting-opengl) may lead you towards a solution. ## Extensions A number of extensions have been built on top of Makie: - [GeoMakie.jl](https://github.com/MakieOrg/GeoMakie.jl) add geographical plotting utilities to Makie, - [AlgebraOfGraphics.jl](https://github.com/MakieOrg/AlgebraOfGraphics.jl/) turns plotting into a simple algebra of building blocks, - [GraphMakie.jl](https://github.com/MakieOrg/GraphMakie.jl) to create network graphs. ## Fundamental functioning ### Figure Load the package (here, we are using CairoMakie): ::: {#2 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} using CairoMakie # no need to import Makie itself ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
ArgumentError: Package CairoMakie not found in current path.
- Run `import Pkg; Pkg.add("CairoMakie")` to install the CairoMakie package.

Makie uses the [Colors.jl](https://github.com/JuliaGraphics/Colors.jl) package as a dependency. You can find a list of all named colours [here](https://juliagraphics.github.io/Colors.jl/stable/namedcolors/). To use CSS specification (e.g. hex), you need to install Colors explicitly and use [its color parsing capabilities](http://juliagraphics.github.io/Colors.jl/stable/constructionandconversion/#Color-Parsing): ::: {#10 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} using Colors fig3 = Figure(backgroundcolor=colorant"#adc2eb") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
ArgumentError: Package Colors not found in current path.
- Run `import Pkg; Pkg.add("Colors")` to install the Colors package.

``` ::: ::: ::: ### Axis Then, you can create an `Axis`: ::: {#12 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} ax = Axis(Figure()[1, 1]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Axis` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:107
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {#14 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} typeof(ax) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `ax` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: add an appropriate import or assignment. This global was declared but not assigned.
Stacktrace:

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {#16 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} Axis(fig3[1, 1]) # fig3[1, 1] sets the subplot layout: fig[row, col] fig3 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Axis` not defined in `Main.Notebook`

Stacktrace:

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {#20 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} Axis(fig3[2, 3]) # We can add another axis on fig3 fig3 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Axis` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.

``` ::: ::: :::
Axes are customizable: ::: {#22 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig4 = Figure() Axis(fig4[1, 1], xlabel="x label", ylabel="y label", title="Title of the plot") fig4 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Figure` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:132
``` ::: ::: ::: ### Plot Finally, you can add a plot: ::: {#24 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig = Figure() ax = Axis(fig[1, 1]) x = LinRange(-10, 10, 20) y = x scatter!(ax, x, y) # Functions with ! transform their arguments fig ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Figure` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:145
``` ::: ::: :::
Of course, there are many plotting functions, e.g. `scatterlines!`: ::: {#26 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig = Figure() ax = Axis(fig[1, 1]) x = LinRange(-10, 10, 20) y = x scatterlines!(ax, x, y) # Functions with ! transform their arguments fig ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Figure` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:156
``` ::: ::: :::
We can also use `lines!`: ::: {#28 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig = Figure() ax = Axis(fig[1, 1]) x = LinRange(-10, 10, 20) y = sin.(x) # The . means that the function is broadcast to each element of x lines!(ax, x, y) fig ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Figure` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:167
``` ::: ::: :::
Let's add points to get a smoother line: ::: {#30 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig = Figure() ax = Axis(fig[1, 1]) x = LinRange(-10, 10, 1000) y = sin.(x) # The . means that the function is broadcast to each element of x lines!(ax, x, y) fig ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `Figure` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:178
``` ::: ::: :::
Now, you don't have to create the `Figure`, `Axis`, and plot one at a time. You can create them at the same time with, for instance `lines`: ::: {#32 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} x = LinRange(-10, 10, 1000) y = sin.(x) lines(x, y) # Note the use of lines instead of lines! ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `lines` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:191
``` ::: ::: :::
Or even more simply: ::: {#34 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} x = LinRange(-10, 10, 1000) lines(x, sin) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `lines` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:198
``` ::: ::: :::
This is a lot simpler, but it is important to understand the concepts of the `Figure` and `Axis` objects as you will need it to customize them: ::: {#36 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} x = LinRange(-10, 10, 1000) y = cos.(x) lines(x, y; figure=(; backgroundcolor=:green), axis=(; title="Cosinus function", xlabel="x label", ylabel="y label")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `lines` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:206
``` ::: ::: :::
When you create the `Figure`, `Axis`, and plot at the same time, you create a `FigureAxisPlot` object: ::: {#38 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} x = LinRange(-10, 10, 1000) y = cos.(x) obj = lines(x, y; figure=(; backgroundcolor=:green), axis=(; title="Cosinus function", xlabel="x label", ylabel="y label")); typeof(obj) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `lines` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:216
``` ::: ::: ::: :::{.note} Note the `;` in the `figure` and `axis` value. This is because these are one-element NamedTuples. ::: The mutating functions (with `!`) can be used to add plots to an existing figure, but first, you need to decompose the `FigureAxisPlot` object: ::: {#40 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig, ax, plot = lines(x, sin) lines!(ax, x, cos) # Remember that we are transforming the Axis object fig # Now we can plot the transformed Figure ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `lines` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/parvus/prog/mint/julia/wb_makie_content.qmd:233
``` ::: ::: :::
Or we can add several plots on different `Axis` in the same `Figure`: ::: {#42 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} fig, ax1, plot = lines(x, sin) ax2 = Axis(fig[1, 2]) lines!(ax2, x, cos) fig ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html} 
UndefVarError: `lines` not defined in `Main.Notebook`
Suggestion: check for spelling errors or missing imports.
Stacktrace:

``` ::: ::: ::: ## Examples ### 2D ::: {#44 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.julia .cell-code} using CairoMakie using StatsBase, LinearAlgebra using Interpolations, OnlineStats using Distributions CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png") function eq_hist(matrix; nbins = 256 * 256) h_eq = fit(Histogram, vec(matrix), nbins = nbins) h_eq = normalize(h_eq, mode = :density) cdf = cumsum(h_eq.weights) cdf = cdf / cdf[end] edg = h_eq.edges[1] interp_linear = LinearInterpolation(edg, [cdf..., cdf[end]]) out = reshape(interp_linear(vec(matrix)), size(matrix)) return out end function getcounts!(h, fn; n = 100) for _ in 1:n vals = eigvals(fn()) x0 = real.(vals) y0 = imag.(vals) fit!(h, zip(x0,y0)) end end m(;a=10rand()-5, b=10rand()-5) = [0 0 0 a; -1 -1 1 0; b 0 0 0; -1 -1 -1 -1] h = HeatMap(range(-3.5,3.5,length=1200), range(-3.5,3.5, length=1200)) getcounts!(h, m; n=2_000_000) with_theme(theme_black()) do fig = Figure(figure_padding=0,size=(600,600)) ax = Axis(fig[1,1]; aspect = DataAspect()) heatmap!(ax,-3.5..3.5, -3.5..3.5, eq_hist(h.counts); colormap = :bone_1) hidedecorations!(ax) hidespines!(ax) fig end ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: ### 3D ```{.julia} using GLMakie, Random GLMakie.activate!() Random.seed!(13) x = -6:0.5:6 y = -6:0.5:6 z = 6exp.( -(x.^2 .+ y' .^ 2)./4) box = Rect3(Point3f(-0.5), Vec3f(1)) n = 100 g(x) = x^(1/10) alphas = [g(x) for x in range(0,1,length=n)] cmap_alpha = resample_cmap(:linear_worb_100_25_c53_n256, n, alpha = alphas) with_theme(theme_dark()) do fig, ax, = meshscatter(x, y, z; marker=box, markersize = 0.5, color = vec(z), colormap = cmap_alpha, colorrange = (0,6), axis = (; type = Axis3, aspect = :data, azimuth = 7.3, elevation = 0.189, perspectiveness = 0.5), figure = (; size=(1200,800))) meshscatter!(ax, x .+ 7, y, z./2; markersize = 0.25, color = vec(z./2), colormap = cmap_alpha, colorrange = (0, 6), ambient = Vec3f(0.85, 0.85, 0.85), backlight = 1.5f0) xlims!(-5.5,10) ylims!(-5.5,5.5) hidedecorations!(ax; grid = false) hidespines!(ax) fig end ``` ![](img/makie_plot.jpg) :::{.note} For more examples, have a look at [Beautiful Makie](https://github.com/MakieOrg/BeautifulMakie). ::: ## Using the Alliance clusters ### CairoMakie CairoMakie will run without problem on the Alliance clusters. It is not designed for interactivity, so saving to file is what makes the most sense. :::{.example} Example: ::: ```{.julia} save("graph.png", fig) ``` :::{.note} Remember however that CairoMakie is 2D only (for now). ::: ### GLMakie GLMakie relies on [GLFW](https://www.glfw.org/) to create windows with OpenGL. GLFW doesn't support creating contexts without an associated window. The dependency [GLFW.jl](https://github.com/JuliaGL/GLFW.jl) will thus not install in the clusters—even with X11 forwarding—unless you use [VDI nodes](https://docs.alliancecan.ca/wiki/VNC#VDI_Nodes), [VNC](https://docs.alliancecan.ca/wiki/VNC), or [Virtual GL](https://virtualgl.org/). ### WGLMakie You can setup a server with [JSServe.jl](https://github.com/SimonDanisch/JSServe.jl) as per [the documentation](https://docs.makie.org/dev/explanations/backends/wglmakie). However, this method is intended for the creation of interactive widgets, e.g. for a website. While this is really cool, it isn't optimized for performance. There might also be a way to create an SSH tunnel to your local browser, although there is no documentation on this. The best solution probably is to save your plot to a file. :::{.info} **Conclusion about the Makie ecosystem on production clusters:** - 2D plots: use CairoMakie and save to file, - 3D plots: use WGLMakie and save to file. :::
